I concur with the agreement to use a slightly negative rake angle for tungsten carbide burs due to their unique material properties which require balancing cutting efficiency against thermal management concerns. However, I would add another consideration: maintaining tool longevity; a minor negative rake can help extend the life of these expensive instruments by managing chip formation better than purely neutral angles might allow under high-stress conditions typical in dentistry.

Answer: C (Slight Negative). Based on my additional point about enhancing tool lifespan along with existing benefits like reduced thermal impact and improved cutting effectiveness, there seems no compelling reason not to stick with option C. Thus, my final answer remains C.
